title: U.S. Route 15 in New York

infobox.value: [I-99](/wiki/Interstate_99_(Pennsylvania) "Interstate 99 (Pennsylvania)") / [US 15](/wiki/U.S._Route_15_in_Pennsylvania "U.S. Route 15 in Pennsylvania") at the [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") state line in [Lawrenceville, PA](/wiki/Lawrenceville,_Pennsylvania "Lawrenceville, Pennsylvania")

[I-86](/wiki/Interstate_86_(New_York) "Interstate 86 (New York)") / [I-99](/wiki/Interstate_99_(New_York) "Interstate 99 (New York)") / [NY 17](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17 "New York State Route 17") / [NY 352](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_352 "New York State Route 352") near [Painted Post](/wiki/Painted_Post,_New_York "Painted Post, New York")

* [Interstate](/wiki/List_of_Interstate_Highways_in_New_York "List of Interstate Highways in New York") * [US](/wiki/List_of_U.S._Routes_in_New_York "List of U.S. Routes in New York") * [State](/wiki/List_of_state_routes_in_New_York "List of state routes in New York") * [Reference](/wiki/List_of_reference_routes_in_New_York "List of reference routes in New York") * [Parkways](/wiki/Parkways_in_New_York "Parkways in New York") | |

(**US 15**) is a part of the [United States Numbered Highway System](/wiki/United_States_Numbered_Highway_System "United States Numbered Highway System") that runs from [Walterboro, South Carolina](/wiki/Walterboro,_South_Carolina "Walterboro, South Carolina"), to [Painted Post, New York](/wiki/Painted_Post,_New_York "Painted Post, New York"). In the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), US 15 extends 12.68 miles (20.41 km) through the [Southern Tier](/wiki/Southern_Tier "Southern Tier") from the [Pennsylvania state line](/wiki/New_York%E2%80%93Pennsylvania_border "New York–Pennsylvania border") at [Lindley](/wiki/Lindley,_New_York "Lindley, New York") north to an interchange with the [Southern Tier Expressway](/wiki/Southern_Tier_Expressway "Southern Tier Expressway") ([I-86](/wiki/Interstate_86_(Pennsylvania%E2%80%93New_York) "Interstate 86 (Pennsylvania–New York)")/[NY 17](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17 "New York State Route 17")) just outside Painted Post. US 15 originally continued north to [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York") before being truncated to its present northern terminus. All of US 15 in New York was designated as **Interstate 99** (**I-99**) on June 27, 2014, and US 15 now runs concurrently with I-99.[[3]](#cite_note-I99NY-3)

(**NY 4**), a highway extending from the [Pennsylvania state line](/wiki/New_York%E2%80%93Pennsylvania_border "New York–Pennsylvania border") in [Lindley](/wiki/Lindley,_New_York "Lindley, New York") to downtown [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York").[[6]](#cite_note-6) NY 4 roughly followed the modern alignment of US 15 from [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") to Presho, Indian Hill Road from Presho to Erwin, [NY 417](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_417 "New York State Route 417") from Erwin to [Gang Mills](/wiki/Gang_Mills,_New_York "Gang Mills, New York"), and Hamilton Street from Gang Mills to [Painted Post](/wiki/Painted_Post,_New_York "Painted Post, New York"). From Painted Post northward, NY 4 was routed along modern [NY 415](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_415 "New York State Route 415") to [Wayland](/wiki/Wayland_(village),_New_York "Wayland (village), New York"), from where it continued to Rochester on what is now [NY 15](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_15 "New York State Route 15"). At the state line, NY 4 connected to [Pennsylvania Route 4](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Route_4_(1920s) "Pennsylvania Route 4 (1920s)") (PA 4).[[7]](#cite_note-7) In 1927, [US 4](/wiki/U.S._Route_4_in_New_York "U.S. Route 4 in New York") was first signed in New York. As a result, NY 4 was renumbered to **New York State Route 2** (**NY 2**) to eliminate numerical duplication with the new U.S. Highway.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Segment of American highway

This article is about the section of U.S. Route 15 in New York. For the entire route, see [U.S. Route 15](/wiki/U.S._Route_15 "U.S. Route 15").

"Interstate 99 in New York" redirects here. For the entire length of the highway, see [Interstate 99](/wiki/Interstate_99 "Interstate 99").

**U.S. Route 15** (**US 15**) is a part of the [United States Numbered Highway System](/wiki/United_States_Numbered_Highway_System "United States Numbered Highway System") that runs from [Walterboro, South Carolina](/wiki/Walterboro,_South_Carolina "Walterboro, South Carolina"), to [Painted Post, New York](/wiki/Painted_Post,_New_York "Painted Post, New York"). In the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), US 15 extends 12.68 miles (20.41 km) through the [Southern Tier](/wiki/Southern_Tier "Southern Tier") from the [Pennsylvania state line](/wiki/New_York%E2%80%93Pennsylvania_border "New York–Pennsylvania border") at [Lindley](/wiki/Lindley,_New_York "Lindley, New York") north to an interchange with the [Southern Tier Expressway](/wiki/Southern_Tier_Expressway "Southern Tier Expressway") ([I-86](/wiki/Interstate_86_(Pennsylvania%E2%80%93New_York) "Interstate 86 (Pennsylvania–New York)")/[NY 17](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17 "New York State Route 17")) just outside Painted Post. US 15 originally continued north to [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York") before being truncated to its present northern terminus. All of US 15 in New York was designated as **Interstate 99** (**I-99**) on June 27, 2014, and US 15 now runs concurrently with I-99.[[3]](#cite_note-I99NY-3)

## Route description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_15_in_New_York&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Route description")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/US_15_south_at_Presho_NY_exit.jpg/250px-US_15_south_at_Presho_NY_exit.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_15_south_at_Presho_NY_exit.jpg)

Looking southward along US 15 from the Smith Road overpass in Presho prior to the road's completion. The highway previously narrowed from four to two lanes in the background.

I-99/US 15 enters New York adjacent to the state line, borough of [Lawrenceville, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Lawrenceville,_Pennsylvania "Lawrenceville, Pennsylvania"). A four-lane freeway through the [Steuben County](/wiki/Steuben_County,_New_York "Steuben County, New York") town of [Lindley](/wiki/Lindley,_New_York "Lindley, New York"), I-99/US 15 crosses through a rock cut, making a large bend to the north and bypassing the hamlet of [Presho](/wiki/Presho,_New_York "Presho, New York"). I-99/US 15 enters a [partial cloverleaf interchange](/wiki/Partial_cloverleaf_interchange "Partial cloverleaf interchange") with [County Route 5](/wiki/County_Route_5_(Steuben_County,_New_York) "County Route 5 (Steuben County, New York)") (CR 5; Smith Road). After CR 5, I-99/US 15 bends northeast through the town of [Erwin](/wiki/Erwin,_New_York "Erwin, New York"), running to the west of the Indian Hills Golf Club. Making a gradual bend further to the northeast, the freeway crosses the [Canisteo River](/wiki/Canisteo_River "Canisteo River") and enters the hamlet of Erwin, where it enters a [diamond interchange](/wiki/Diamond_interchange "Diamond interchange") with [NY 417](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_417 "New York State Route 417") (Addison Road). After NY 417, I-99/US 15 makes a bend alongside [Norfolk Southern Railroad](/wiki/Norfolk_Southern_Railway "Norfolk Southern Railway")'s [Southern Tier Line](/wiki/Southern_Tier_Line "Southern Tier Line") (former [Erie Railroad](/wiki/Erie_Railroad "Erie Railroad") main line). Now paralleling the tracks and NY 417, I-99/US 15 crosses through Erwin, entering exit 3, which connects to NY 417 once again, next to Gang Mills Yard,[[4]](#cite_note-bing-4) the site of the former [Painted Post](/w/index.php?title=Painted_Post_(Erie_Railroad_station)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Painted Post (Erie Railroad station) (page does not exist)") station.[[5]](#cite_note-yanosey-5)

After Gang Mills Yard, I-99/US 15 crosses through the [Gang Mills](/wiki/Gang_Mills,_New_York "Gang Mills, New York") section of Erwin, entering a large interchange at the northern end of the neighborhood. Signed exit 12, this interchange serves [CR 107](/wiki/County_Route_107_(Steuben_County,_New_York) "County Route 107 (Steuben County, New York)") (Robert Dann Drive) via NY 417. After CR 107, I-99/US 15 enters a large interchange that utilizes several flyover ramps between I-99/US 15, [I-86](/wiki/Interstate_86_(Pennsylvania%E2%80%93New_York) "Interstate 86 (Pennsylvania–New York)"), and [NY 17](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17 "New York State Route 17") ([Southern Tier Expressway](/wiki/Southern_Tier_Expressway "Southern Tier Expressway")). Ramps are also present, connecting to [NY 352](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_352 "New York State Route 352"). This interchange serves as the northern terminus of I-99/US 15.[[4]](#cite_note-bing-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_15_in_New_York&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### Origins and designation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_15_in_New_York&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Origins and designation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/US_15_in_Rochester_NY.jpg/250px-US_15_in_Rochester_NY.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_15_in_Rochester_NY.jpg)

Authentic US 15 shield at the former northern terminus in Rochester.

The first set of posted routes in [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") was assigned in 1924. One route assigned at this time was **New York State Route 4** (**NY 4**), a highway extending from the [Pennsylvania state line](/wiki/New_York%E2%80%93Pennsylvania_border "New York–Pennsylvania border") in [Lindley](/wiki/Lindley,_New_York "Lindley, New York") to downtown [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York").[[6]](#cite_note-6) NY 4 roughly followed the modern alignment of US 15 from [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") to Presho, Indian Hill Road from Presho to Erwin, [NY 417](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_417 "New York State Route 417") from Erwin to [Gang Mills](/wiki/Gang_Mills,_New_York "Gang Mills, New York"), and Hamilton Street from Gang Mills to [Painted Post](/wiki/Painted_Post,_New_York "Painted Post, New York"). From Painted Post northward, NY 4 was routed along modern [NY 415](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_415 "New York State Route 415") to [Wayland](/wiki/Wayland_(village),_New_York "Wayland (village), New York"), from where it continued to Rochester on what is now [NY 15](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_15 "New York State Route 15"). At the state line, NY 4 connected to [Pennsylvania Route 4](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Route_4_(1920s) "Pennsylvania Route 4 (1920s)") (PA 4).[[7]](#cite_note-7) In 1927, [US 4](/wiki/U.S._Route_4_in_New_York "U.S. Route 4 in New York") was first signed in New York. As a result, NY 4 was renumbered to **New York State Route 2** (**NY 2**) to eliminate numerical duplication with the new U.S. Highway.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

By 1929, [US 111](/wiki/U.S._Route_111 "U.S. Route 111") was assigned, following what had been PA 4 northward from [Williamsport, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Williamsport,_Pennsylvania "Williamsport, Pennsylvania"), to the New York state line at [Lawrenceville](/wiki/Lawrenceville,_Pennsylvania "Lawrenceville, Pennsylvania"), where it ended.[[9]](#cite_note-9) At some point between 1935 and 1937,[[10]](#cite_note-1935map-10)[[11]](#cite_note-1937map-11) [US 15](/wiki/U.S._Route_15_in_Pennsylvania "U.S. Route 15 in Pennsylvania") was extended northward from [Harrisburg, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Harrisburg,_Pennsylvania "Harrisburg, Pennsylvania"),[[12]](#cite_note-12) to the New York state line by way of an [overlap](/wiki/Concurrency_(road) "Concurrency (road)") with US 111.[[10]](#cite_note-1935map-10)[[11]](#cite_note-1937map-11) The overlap between US 15 and US 111 was eliminated c. 1938[[13]](#cite_note-1938map-13)[[11]](#cite_note-1937map-11) when US 111 was truncated southward to Harrisburg.[[14]](#cite_note-14) US 15 was extended northward to Rochester by the following April, replacing all of NY 2.[[1]](#cite_note-1939changes-1)

### Realignments and truncation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_15_in_New_York&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Realignments and truncation")]

Construction began c. 1962 on a bypass of [NY 17](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17 "New York State Route 17") and US 15 in the vicinity of Corning (modern exit 45) and Painted Post (exit 43).[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) The entirety of the highway, plus an extension northwest to [Campbell](/wiki/Campbell,_New_York "Campbell, New York") (exit 41), was completed between 1964 and 1968. US 15 and NY 17 were rerouted to follow the new highway, and their former routings between Campbell and Corning were redesignated as [NY 415](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_415 "New York State Route 415").[[17]](#cite_note-1964map-17)[[18]](#cite_note-1968map-18) The portion of the US 15/NY 17 freeway between Campbell and Avoca (exit 36) was completed by 1973.[[19]](#cite_note-19) On July 1, 1974, US 15 was truncated to its current northern terminus in Painted Post and replaced with NY 15 from Painted Post to Rochester.[[20]](#cite_note-1974log-20)

In the mid-1960s, work began on a limited-access highway paralleling US 15 from Presho to the [Southern Tier Expressway](/wiki/Southern_Tier_Expressway "Southern Tier Expressway") at Painted Post.[[17]](#cite_note-1964map-17)[[18]](#cite_note-1968map-18) The highway was completed and opened to traffic c. 1969 as a realignment of US 15. The interchange between US 15 and NY 17 (now concurrent with [I-86](/wiki/Interstate_86_(Pennsylvania%E2%80%93New_York) "Interstate 86 (Pennsylvania–New York)")) in Painted Post has been redesigned twice. The original configuration of the interchange featured a loop centered around the point where US 15 met NY 17, with ramps providing access to and from the loop from US 15, NY 17, and North Hamilton Street.[[18]](#cite_note-1968map-18)[[21]](#cite_note-21) The second design, in place until 2003, was a semi-[diamond interchange](/wiki/Diamond_interchange "Diamond interchange"), with US 15 intersecting ramps from I-86 and NY 17 at-grade. One connection, I-86/NY 17 east to North Hamilton Street, was lost in this version due to the ramp setup of the southern half of the interchange. North of the ramps leading to and from I-86/NY 17 westbound, the [right-of-way](/wiki/Right-of-way_(transportation) "Right-of-way (transportation)") of US 15 continued north into Painted Post as North Hamilton Street.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The current setup is a [directional T interchange](/wiki/Directional_T_interchange "Directional T interchange"), which creates a freeway-to-freeway connection between US 15 and I-86/NY 17. Separate ramps for North Hamilton Street from I-86/NY 17 were retained; however, for the first time, there was no direct connection between US 15 and North Hamilton Street.[[23]](#cite_note-PaintedPostHA-23) Work on the interchange began in late 2003[[24]](#cite_note-24) and was completed in mid-2008.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### Conversion to freeway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_15_in_New_York&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Conversion to freeway")]

In the [National Highway System Designation Act of 1995](/wiki/National_Highway_System_Designation_Act_of_1995 "National Highway System Designation Act of 1995"), the portion of US 15 in New York was defined as part of [I-99](/wiki/Interstate_99 "Interstate 99").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] I-99 currently ends just shy of [I-80](/wiki/Interstate_80_in_Pennsylvania "Interstate 80 in Pennsylvania"); however, construction is either underway or has been completed on projects to upgrade [US 220](/wiki/U.S._Route_220_(Pennsylvania) "U.S. Route 220 (Pennsylvania)") and US 15 between [Bellefonte, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Bellefonte,_Pennsylvania "Bellefonte, Pennsylvania"), and Corning to [Interstate Highway standards](/wiki/Interstate_Highway_standards "Interstate Highway standards").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In June 2007, work began on a new freeway alignment for US 15 between the Pennsylvania state line and Watson Creek Road in Lindley.[[26]](#cite_note-26) The highway was opened to traffic on October 1, 2008.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Ownership and maintenance of the former surface alignment of US 15 was transferred to [Steuben County](/wiki/Steuben_County,_New_York "Steuben County, New York"), which designated the highway as [CR 115](/wiki/County_Route_115_(Steuben_County,_New_York) "County Route 115 (Steuben County, New York)").[[28]](#cite_note-inventory-28)

Ground was broken on September 21, 2009, on the final section of the freeway between Watson Creek Road and Presho. The southbound lanes of the highway opened on August 22, 2013.[[29]](#cite_note-29) The northbound lanes opened on October 8.[[30]](#cite_note-30) The bridge portion of the project received $9 million (equivalent to $12.1 million in 2024[[31]](#cite_note-inflation-USGDP-31)) in annual [Appalachian Development Highway System](/wiki/Appalachian_Development_Highway_System "Appalachian Development Highway System") (ADHS) funding from 2010 to 2012 and $5.7 million (equivalent to $7.68 million in 2024[[31]](#cite_note-inflation-USGDP-31)) from the state of New York. Local officials had called on state and federal officials to expedite the construction process by paying for the project up front and using ADHS grants in the years to come to pay off the debt later on.[[32]](#cite_note-32) The highway was nominated for a [Transportation Investment Generating Economic Recovery](/wiki/Transportation_Investment_Generating_Economic_Recovery "Transportation Investment Generating Economic Recovery") grant but was rejected.[[33]](#cite_note-finger-33) On June 27, 2014, Governor [Andrew Cuomo](/wiki/Andrew_Cuomo "Andrew Cuomo") announced that US 15 was now designated officially as part of I-99.[[3]](#cite_note-I99NY-3) While signs for US 15 were removed after the designation of I-99, they were restored in 2016, and US 15 and I-99 continue to officially overlap for the entire length of the routes in New York.[[34]](#cite_note-34)
